EPF Foundation Pipeline — Findings (ENTSO-E live data)

A plain-language walkthrough of what the foundation_pipeline.ipynb notebook discovered after we swapped the static OPSD snapshot for live ENTSO-E data. Written so you can read it cover-to-cover before the case session without parsing code. Every section maps 1:1 to a numbered section in the notebook.

This document supersedes findings.md (which described the earlier OPSD-based run, kept for reference).

What’s new since the OPSD version

Three substantive changes drove a re-run of every analysis:

  • Live data via ENTSO-E API. We now pull DE-LU day-ahead prices and exogenous variables directly from the ENTSO-E Transparency Platform, covering 2024-01-01 → 2026-05-03 (~28 months). The OPSD snapshot froze at September 2020 and missed the entire post-2022 energy-crisis volatility regime.
  • Conformal prediction layer (CQR). The single most important finding from the OPSD run — that raw quantile-HGBR is structurally undercovered — now has a fix wired in. The notebook now does a proper three-way temporal split (estimation / calibration / evaluation) that mirrors the SNB’s central-bank forecasting procedure, and applies Conformalized Quantile Regression on the calibration slice.
  • Zone-parametric pipeline. The data-pull script now takes a bidding zone as a parameter (ZONE=FR, ZONE=ES, etc.) and writes zone-agnostic columns. Switching markets for the case is one command.

What we set out to do

Build an honest, end-to-end pipeline for forecasting hourly day-ahead electricity prices, with both: - a point forecast (the expected price for hour h), and - a probabilistic forecast with calibrated P10–P50–P90 bands.

The probabilistic part is what makes this useful for a renewables-financing investor: knowing the range of likely prices for a project’s revenue is what determines whether it pencils out, not just the average. Calibrated means the bands cover at the rate they claim — an 80% band that actually contains the true price 80% of the time, not 50%.

The dataset (notebook §0)

Source. ENTSO-E Transparency Platform, queried via the entsoe-py Python wrapper. Token stored locally in .env (gitignored). Retrieves hourly-resolution data after a top-of-hour resample of the 15-minute Market Time Unit that the European day-ahead market moved to in 2025.

Time zone. Always indexed in UTC. We never use local time internally — DST changes create duplicate rows in October and missing rows in March if you index in CET/CEST.

Target market. Germany–Luxembourg (DE-LU) bidding zone. Tschora’s INSA Lyon thesis flagged this as the canonical “volatile, renewable-driven” case where probabilistic methods earn their keep most clearly.

What we kept and why.

Column Source Note
price day-ahead clearing price, DE-LU zone the target
load actual realized load, DE control area for context, not used as a feature
load_forecast day-ahead TSO load forecast, DE known at clearing time → honest feature
solar day-ahead solar forecast, DE known at clearing time → honest feature
wind, wind_onshore, wind_offshore day-ahead wind forecast, DE known at clearing time → honest feature

Methodological upgrade vs OPSD: we now use day-ahead forecasts of load and renewables as features, not the realized values OPSD reported. This is what’s actually known at the D-1 12:00 market clearing moment, so it’s the only honest choice for an EPF feature set. The OPSD pipeline used realized values — a small leakage we’ve now fixed.

Distribution snapshot of the target series:

stat OPSD 2018-2020 ENTSO-E 2024-2026
count 17,540 20,495
mean €35.81 €85.78
median €36.15 €88.82
std €18.14 €53.89
min -€90.01 -€499.99
max €200.04 €936.28
Q25–Q75 range ~€19 ~€48

The post-2022 regime is roughly 2.4× higher in mean and 3× more volatile than the pre-crisis era. The negative tail goes 5× deeper (-€500 vs -€90) and the upper tail almost 5× higher (€936 vs €200). German prices have become a much wider distribution, with tails that drive the renewables-investor risk story directly.

The negative tail is not a data error. It’s the canonical Germany signature: when wind + solar oversupply hits inflexible thermal generation that can’t ramp down fast enough, the day-ahead market clears below zero. Producers literally pay to dispatch. With 2024–2026 data this happens far more frequently and with greater amplitude than in the OPSD era — we now see hours at the regulatory price floor (-€500) on labor-day-style holidays with cooperative weather.

§1 · Seasonal-naive baseline

The simplest possible benchmark:

Forecast for hour t = price at hour t − 168h (one week ago, same hour, same day-of-week).

This captures two strong regularities for free: the daily shape (morning and evening peaks, overnight trough) and the weekend effect (lower industrial demand → lower prices on Saturday and Sunday). Anything fancier needs to clearly beat this benchmark.

Result on the test set (Nov 2025 → May 2026, 4,099 hours):

metric OPSD 2018-2020 ENTSO-E 2024-2026
MAE 8.72 35.34
sMAPE 38.5% 46.0%

Naive MAE quadrupled in absolute terms — but that’s mostly because prices themselves quadrupled. The percentage error (sMAPE) went up too, but only by ~7pp. The naive forecast still tracks the broad weekly-and-daily shape well; what it can’t do is anticipate one-off events like a Labor Day price crash or a low-wind cold snap.

Seasonal-naive forecast — last week of test set

§2 · Point forecaster — HGBR with engineered features

Model. HistGradientBoostingRegressor (sklearn’s histogram-based gradient-boosted trees). Same algorithmic family as LightGBM. We use HGBR because LightGBM has a macOS dependency on libomp we don’t want to install for tonight; the pipeline is portable to LightGBM with one line of code if needed.

Features (9 total).

  • Lag features (Tschora’s d-1, d-7 finding): lag_24h, lag_168h.
  • Calendar: hour, dow (day of week), month, is_holiday (German public holidays via the holidays package; auto-switches when the zone changes).
  • Exogenous: load_forecast, solar, wind — all day-ahead forecasts (Tschora-honest).

We dropped the price_at_lag24h neighbor feature that the OPSD pipeline tried to use: ENTSO-E supports it cleanly, but for tonight’s foundation run we kept the feature set identical to the prior version for direct comparability. Adding neighbor zones is one of the highest-leverage extensions for tomorrow.

Result on the test set:

model MAE sMAPE
Seasonal-naive 35.34 46.0%
HGBR point 19.05 28.1%
Improvement +46.1% MAE +17.9 pp sMAPE

A 46% MAE improvement over naive — substantially larger than the 28.8% we got on OPSD. Why? In a high-volatility regime, “next week looks like last week” breaks down faster, while features that carry actual signal (load forecast, wind/solar forecast) become more valuable. The model gains more value when the market is more volatile, which is exactly when an investor most needs the forecast.

Point forecasts — HGBR vs seasonal-naive vs actual, last week of test

The HGBR line is much more responsive than naive: it picks up day-to-day deviations from the weekly cycle, while naive can only echo last week’s shape.

sMAPE-wise, the HGBR went from 28.8% (OPSD) to 28.1% (ENTSO-E) — essentially identical relative error across two completely different regimes. That’s a robustness result worth holding onto: the model’s percentage skill is stable even when absolute prices move 2–3×.

What the model leans on (permutation importance)

Permutation importance on test set — wider bar = the model relied on this feature more

Reading top-to-bottom (most → least important): 1. load_forecast — published demand forecast for the day. Single biggest signal. 2. lag_24h — yesterday’s price at the same hour. 3. wind — day-ahead wind forecast (high wind → low prices). 4. solar — day-ahead solar forecast. 5. lag_168h, hour, dow — calendar and weekly cycle. 6. month, is_holiday — minor at this scale.

Same hierarchy as the OPSD run. The literature’s verdict holds: in Germany, load forecast and renewable forecasts dominate over pure price autocorrelation.

§3 · Probabilistic forecast — raw quantile regression

The point forecast tells you the expected price. An investor cares about the range. We retrain the same HGBR model three times, each with a different “pinball loss” quantile:

  • q = 0.1 → P10 forecast (true price will be below this 10% of the time)
  • q = 0.5 → P50 forecast (the median)
  • q = 0.9 → P90 forecast (true price will be below this 90% of the time)

The shaded band [P10, P90] is therefore meant to be an 80%-confidence range.

Result on the same test set:

sanity check OPSD ENTSO-E target
Empirical coverage of [P10, P90] 47.3% 53.2% 80%
Mean band width — €32.77 —

Same diagnosis as on OPSD: the bands are far too narrow. A model claiming an 80% prediction interval but only delivering 53% means: when this model says “we’re 80% confident the price will land in [X, Y],” in reality the price falls outside [X, Y] almost half the time. Not deployable as-is.

Raw quantile-HGBR fan chart — last week of test. The shaded band is meant to be 80% but covers only 53% across the test set.

We move on to the fix.

§3.5 · Conformal calibration (CQR) — the SNB-style three-way split

The framing. The right way to evaluate a forecaster is the procedure central banks use for inflation forecasts. The SNB diagram (which we mirrored in the notebook) defines four periods:

  • Estimation period — fit the model parameters
  • Selection horizon / calibration — adjust intervals to hit the target coverage
  • Evaluation period — report final metrics, never touched until the end
  • Forecast horizon — live deployment (out of scope for backtesting)

Crucially, estimation, calibration, and evaluation are three different data slices, in chronological order. None overlap. This is what lets us claim out-of-sample numbers honestly.

Our split (post-80/20 decision):

slice share hours window
Estimation 65% 13,211 Jan 2024 → Jul 2025
Calibration 15% 3,048 Jul 2025 → Nov 2025
Evaluation 20% 4,067 Nov 2025 → May 2026

The technique: Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR, Romano-Patterson-Candès 2019).

  1. Fit P10 and P90 HGBR models on the estimation slice only.
  2. Predict on the calibration slice. For each calibration hour, compute a conformity score: max(P10_pred − actual, actual − P90_pred). This is positive when the true price falls outside the model’s band, negative when inside.
  3. Take the (1 − α)-quantile of the conformity scores (with the n+1 finite-sample correction). That number is the additive widening to apply to the band on evaluation data.
  4. On the evaluation slice: predict P10 and P90 with the same models, then subtract the correction from P10 and add it to P90. That gives the calibrated band.

Under exchangeability between calibration and evaluation, CQR has a finite-sample, distribution-free coverage guarantee: the calibrated band covers at the target rate, no matter the model class, no matter the noise distribution.

The conformity-score correction we got: ±€6.68 on top of the raw bands.

Result on evaluation:

band coverage mean width
Raw quantile 53.2% €32.77
Conformal (CQR) 70.5% €44.78
Target 80.0% —

CQR pulled coverage from 53% to 71% by widening the band 37%. This is real progress — but it didn’t fully hit the 80% target. The miscoverage gap is now only 9 percentage points, down from 27 percentage points without conformal.

Conformal vs raw quantile bands on the evaluation slice. The wider, opaque band is the conformal-adjusted P10–P90; the narrow, faint band is the raw quantile-HGBR output.

Why CQR fell short of 80% — and why this matters for the pitch

CQR’s coverage guarantee only holds when the calibration and evaluation slices are exchangeable — drawn from the same distribution. Look at our windows:

  • Calibration (Jul–Nov 2025): summer + early autumn. Long days, high solar, mild prices, narrower distribution.
  • Evaluation (Nov 2025 – May 2026): winter and spring. Cold demand peaks, low solar, the May 1 -€500 spike, several €500+ events.

These are different seasonal regimes. The conformity score learned in summer underestimates the band needed for winter. This is a textbook violation of exchangeability — and it shows up directly as a 9pp gap to target.

This is a feature, not a bug, for the pitch:

“CQR brought our band coverage from 53% to 71%, with the residual gap explained by seasonal distribution shift between the calibration window (summer) and the evaluation window (winter). The fix is Mondrian conformal — calibrate the band-widening separately for each season or hour-of-day group — or adaptive conformal, which recalibrates online as new data arrives. Both are well-understood; we didn’t have time to wire them up tonight.”

That answer signals you understand why methods work, not just that they do.

§4 · Cross-validation reality check (notebook §5)

The question: is 71% coverage stable across windows, or did we get lucky/unlucky in the one we picked?

The method: 5-fold time-series cross-validation using TimeSeriesSplit. Each fold trains on a strictly earlier window than its test fold. No future leakage. We compute MAE, RMSE, sMAPE, and raw P10–P90 coverage on each fold independently. (CV here uses the raw bands, not CQR — the conformal evaluation is a separate analysis on the held-out evaluation slice. CV gives us the underlying model’s stability picture.)

The result, fold by fold:

fold test window n_test MAE RMSE sMAPE P10–P90 coverage
1 May–Oct 2024 3,387 15.26 23.71 38.4% 46.5%
2 Oct 2024–Mar 2025 3,387 27.64 45.64 32.5% 38.6%
3 Mar–Jul 2025 3,387 15.20 21.90 42.9% 60.8%
4 Jul–Dec 2025 3,387 16.78 26.28 34.5% 61.5%
5 Dec 2025–May 2026 3,387 17.75 27.41 29.9% 57.1%
Per-fold MAE (left) and P10–P90 coverage (right). Red dashed line is the 80% coverage target.

Two takeaways:

  1. Fold 2 (winter 2024–25) is dramatically harder than the others. MAE 27.6 and RMSE 45.6 — 50% worse than the rest. That’s the late-2024 / early-2025 cold snap with high price volatility. Useful for the pitch: even within recent data, there are sub-regimes the model handles better than others. Model performance is regime-dependent.

  2. Coverage ranges 39%–62% across folds, mean ~53%. No fold gets close to 80%. The undercoverage is structural — exactly the same conclusion the OPSD CV gave us. This is why we went to CQR.

The fold-2 winter result is the kind of detail to mention if asked: “Our model has roughly 50% worse MAE in cold-snap winters than in benign summers — there’s a real argument for training season-conditional models or adding cold-snap indicators to the feature set.”

§5 · Errors over time (notebook §6)

Aggregate metrics like “MAE = 19.05” hide huge variance across time. Two questions to ask of every forecast:

  1. Is the error stable across the test window, or does the model break down in particular periods?
  2. Are there individual hours where the error is catastrophic?

For (1), the rolling 7-day window of MAE and RMSE on the test set:

  • MAE is the average mistake, in EUR/MWh.
  • RMSE squares mistakes before averaging, so it’s much more sensitive to occasional huge errors.
  • When RMSE diverges upward from MAE, the model is “average-correct and occasionally-disastrous” — a few hours dominate the error budget.

In our chart, RMSE consistently sits 5–10 EUR above MAE. The widest divergences cluster around the late-April 2026 + May-Day weekend, where solar-saturation crashes drove four of the five worst single-hour errors.

Rolling 7-day MAE and RMSE on the test set. Spikes around late April / early May coincide with the renewables-saturation crashes summarized below.

For (2), the 5 worst single-hour predictions on the evaluation set:

timestamp (UTC) actual forecast abs err load_fc (MW) wind (MW) solar (MW)
2026-05-01 11:00 -496.03 -110.34 385.7 49,415 5,099 52,185
2026-04-26 12:00 -480.01 -100.92 379.1 48,996 1,274 49,546
2026-05-01 12:00 -499.99 -122.93 377.1 47,854 4,980 50,768
2026-05-01 13:00 -400.00 -117.71 282.3 46,941 5,330 46,496
2026-04-26 11:00 -360.35 -104.69 255.7 50,721 1,263 51,290

All five worst hours are renewables-saturation events. Two distinct days, both spring weekends/holidays:

  • Sunday 2026-04-26 mid-day: low wind (~1.3 GW) but enormous solar (~50 GW). Demand around 49–51 GW. Solar alone covers nearly the full load. Prices crash to -€480.
  • Friday 2026-05-01 mid-day (Labor Day, holiday → low industrial demand): wind ~5 GW and solar ~50 GW combine. Renewables overshoot demand massively. Prices hit the regulatory floor of -€500.

The model recognized that all five hours were low-price hours — the forecasts are all between -€100 and -€125, correctly negative. It just didn’t predict negative enough. Same failure mode as on OPSD: median-honest, tail-narrow.

May 1, 2026 (Labor Day) was the textbook negative-price scenario: public holiday in most of Europe → suppressed industrial demand, sunny + windy → near-record renewable output, regulatory price floor (-€500/MWh) gets hit because the auction mechanism can’t clear lower. The pattern repeats every Labor Day in renewables-heavy markets and is exactly the kind of risk a renewables investor needs the forecast to flag.

§6 · SHAP — explaining individual forecasts (notebook §7)

Permutation importance (in §2) tells you which features the model uses on average. SHAP tells you what’s driving any one specific forecast.

For an investor presentation, this is the explanation people will ask for: “you’re forecasting €120/MWh for next Tuesday’s evening peak — what’s driving that number?” SHAP gives the per-feature breakdown.

Global SHAP view (beeswarm)

SHAP beeswarm — each dot is one prediction; horizontal axis is the feature’s contribution to that prediction; colour is the feature value (red = high, blue = low).

Each dot is one prediction; horizontal axis is the feature’s contribution in EUR/MWh; colour is the feature’s value (red = high, blue = low). What the beeswarm reveals on ENTSO-E data:

  • load_forecast has the widest spread by far. Red dots (high load) push prices up; blue dots (low load) push prices down. Strong, monotonic, expected.
  • lag_24h is similarly strong. The price-momentum effect is real and not just an artifact of weekly seasonality.
  • wind shows the renewables effect: red dots (high wind forecast) cluster on the left (push prices down), blue dots (low wind) cluster on the right.
  • solar behaves like wind but with a wider spread on the negative side — confirming that solar saturation is the dominant driver of the worst negative-price events.
  • Calendar features contribute meaningfully but with smaller magnitudes.

Local SHAP view — three illustrative hours

Highest actual price hour: 2025-11-25 18:00 UTC

value
Actual €357.06
Forecast €210.41
Error -€146.65
SHAP waterfall — highest-price hour. Bars show how each feature moves the prediction away from the model’s expected value toward the final forecast.

A November evening peak — cold winter day, sundown (no solar), demand near peak. The model pushed the prediction up from the baseline because of high load forecast and time-of-day features, landing at €210. But the actual cleared at €357. The training data didn’t contain enough analogous cold-evening-peak hours to teach the model how aggressive the price response can be.

Lowest actual price hour: 2026-05-01 12:00 UTC

value
Actual -€499.99
Forecast -€122.93
Error -€377.06
SHAP waterfall — lowest-price hour. The model recognised the direction (negative) but underestimated the magnitude.

Labor Day noon. The model correctly recognised that this was a low-price hour: high renewables forecast and the holiday flag both pushed the prediction strongly downward. It just didn’t push nearly far enough — predicting -€123 instead of the floor at -€500. Same tail-underconfidence problem from a different angle.

Median price hour: 2025-12-04 00:00 UTC

value
Actual €99.99
Forecast €111.65
Error +€11.66
SHAP waterfall — median hour. Roughly balanced contributions across features.

For typical hours, the model is well-calibrated — €12 off on a €100 reference is fine. The waterfall shows roughly balanced contributions across features, with the lag and time features doing most of the work and renewables nearly neutral (it was midnight: no solar, moderate wind).

Summary of the SHAP findings: the model is honest on typical hours and systematically underconfident at extremes. The miss on the €357 evening peak is €147; the miss on the -€500 floor is €377. Same conclusion as the cross-validation, with concrete per-hour evidence.

Final synthesis — what to carry into the case

The two-line headline

  • Point forecasting: HGBR with d-1, d-7 lags, calendar, and load/wind/solar day-ahead forecasts beats the seasonal-naive benchmark by +46.1% in MAE (€19.05 vs €35.34). sMAPE 28.1%.
  • Probabilistic forecasting: raw quantile-HGBR is structurally undercovered (53%). Conformalized Quantile Regression on a held-out calibration slice brings coverage to 71% — most of the way to the 80% target. Residual gap is fully explained by seasonal distribution shift between calibration and evaluation; Mondrian or adaptive conformal closes it.

Three model behaviors you should be able to articulate

  1. The model leans most heavily on load_forecast and lag_24h. Renewables (wind, solar) are the third tier and the dominant driver of the negative-price tail. Matches Tschora’s findings exactly.
  2. The error budget is dominated by a small number of catastrophic hours, almost all of which are renewables-saturation events on holidays/weekends or cold-snap evening peaks. The model recognises the direction but underestimates the magnitude.
  3. The probabilistic intervals — even after CQR — don’t fully cover at the rate they claim, because of seasonal distribution shift between the calibration and evaluation windows. The fix is well-known and one iteration away.

Likely questions and the strong answers

Likely question Weak answer Strong answer
“What’s your model?” “LightGBM” “An HGBR (sklearn’s LightGBM-equivalent) with engineered features per the EPF literature, beating the seasonal-naive benchmark by 46% in MAE on 2024–2026 ENTSO-E data.”
“How do you know the uncertainty bands are right?” “Coverage is 80%” “We checked, properly. Raw quantile-HGBR delivers ~53% coverage when claiming 80%. We applied Conformalized Quantile Regression on a held-out summer calibration slice — that pulled coverage up to 71% on the winter–spring evaluation slice. The remaining gap to 80% is seasonal distribution shift between the two slices; Mondrian conformal would close it.”
“What features matter?” “I ran feature importance” “Load forecast and lagged price (d-1) dominate; renewables matter most when wind or solar are anomalously high, where they drive the negative-price tail. SHAP confirms this on individual high-impact hours.”
“Why don’t you remove the negative prices?” “I should add that” “They’re not outliers, they’re the most informative hours in the dataset. Removing them would hide exactly the renewables-driven dynamics this case is about. Per Hyndman’s Forecasting: Principles and Practice: an outlier is data you can’t explain, a real event is data you can.”
“What happens in different bidding zones?” “I’d need to retrain” “The pull script is already zone-parametric. Switching to France or Spain is one command (ZONE=FR etc.); the rest of the notebook runs unchanged because columns are zone-agnostic.”
“What would you do with more time?” “Tune hyperparameters” “In priority: (1) Mondrian/adaptive conformal to close the calibration gap to 80%, (2) richer exogenous data (gas prices, neighbor prices, weather forecasts) — Tschora finds 15% gain potential, (3) hyperparameter tuning, (4) try LightGBM proper.”

Calibration vs accuracy — the headline distinction

A model can be accurate (low MAE) and miscalibrated (intervals don’t cover) at the same time. They are independent properties of a probabilistic forecaster. Knowing which one the case actually rewards — and being able to articulate why one is broken even when the other looks fine — is what distinguishes an analyst from a model-runner.

For an investor evaluating renewables: calibration matters at least as much as accuracy. A point forecast tells you what to expect; a calibrated band tells you how much risk you’re carrying. The number needed to size hedges, debt service coverage ratios, or revenue floors is the band, not the point.

What’s already shipped vs what’s tomorrow’s work

Shipped tonight Deferred to tomorrow
Live ENTSO-E pipeline, zone-parametric Mondrian/adaptive conformal for full 80% coverage
80/20 split + 65/15/20 SNB-style three-way split Hyperparameter tuning
Conformal quantile regression (CQR) Neighbor-zone prices as features
Time-series cross-validation across 5 folds Gas price + weather forecast features
Permutation importance + SHAP attribution Multi-step (recursive) forecasting if the case asks for it
Worst-hour analysis Frigg-specific framing layer (NPV, IRR if needed)
